turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has our opinions this morning in two cases.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I will start with Honeycutt versus United States. A federal statute codified 21 U.S.C. §853, mandates forfeiture of “any property constituting or derived from any proceeds the person obtained directly or indirectly as a result of certain crimes.” This case concerns how the statute operates when two or more defendants act as part of a conspiracy. The precise issue is whether §853 renders the defendant jointly and severally liable for property that his co-conspirator gained from the crime but that the defendant himself did not acquire. We hold that it does not. Terry Honeycutt managed sales and inventory for a hardware store owned by his brother Tony Honeycutt. The store sold a water purification product sometimes used to manufacture meth. The brothers were indicted for federal drug crimes including conspiracy to distribute a product used in that production. Pursuant to §853, the Government sought judgment against each brother for the full amount of the profits from the sale of the product. Tony agreed to forfeit $200,000.00 of that amount. The Government looked to Terry for the remaining $69,000.00. Despite conceding that Terry did not benefit personally from the product sales, the Government asked the district court to hold him jointly and severally liable for the full amount of the profits from the illegal sales. The district court declined to enter a forfeiture judgment against Terry, reasoning that he was a salaried employee who had not received any profits from the sale. The Sixth Circuit reversed holding that the brothers as co-conspirators, were jointly and severally liable for any conspiracy proceeds. We granted certiorari and now reverse. Forfeiture under §853 applies broadly to any person convicted of certain drug crimes, but §853(a) limits the reach of the statue by defining the property subject to forfeiture. It restricts forfeiture to tainted property flowing from or used in the crime. It also defines forfeitable property solely in terms of personal possession or use. §853(a)(1), the provision at issue here limits forfeiture to the property that defendant obtained as the result of the crime. Neither the dictionary definition nor the common use of the word obtained supports the conclusion that an individual obtains property that was acquired by someone else. Joint and several liability instead would require a forfeiture of untainted property that the defendant never acquired and is therefore inconsistent with §853(a). Joint and several liability would also render futile §853(p), which is the sole provision of §853 that permits the Government to confiscate property untainted by the crime. Under §853(p), the Government may forfeit substitute that is untainted property only if they can prove that the defendant himself caused the loss or dissipation of the tainted property he once acquired. Reading the statue to require defendants who never obtained tainted property to forfeit substitute untainted property would allow circumvention of Congress’ carefully constructed statutory scheme. We decline to the so. For these another reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit. The decision of the Court is unanimous, except that Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
